title: How the adrenal glands burn out from decades of hypervigilance
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
When your body's alarm system never gets to rest

You know that feeling. The one where you're waiting for the other shoe to drop. Where your shoulders live somewhere up near your ears. Where your jaw is clenched and you didn't even notice until someone asked if you were okay. That's hypervigilance. And it's not just a mental state. It's a full body event.

I spent years thinking I was just "high strung" or "anxious by nature." I'd wake up already tired. My muscles ached for no reason I could name. I'd get sick every few months and take forever to recover. And I kept wondering why. Why couldn't I just relax? Why did my body feel like it was bracing for impact even when nothing bad was happening?

Here's the thing. Your adrenal glands are these tiny little organs that sit on top of your kidneys. They're about the size of a walnut. And they're responsible for producing cortisol, adrenaline, and norepinephrine. Those are your stress hormones. The ones that help you run from danger. The ones that keep you alive when something is actually threatening you.

But here's where it gets complicated. When you've lived through chronic trauma, especially developmental trauma that happened before you had words for it, your nervous system learns that the world is not safe. It learns that danger is constant. That threat is everywhere. And it keeps your adrenal glands working overtime. Day after day. Year after year.

Peter Levine, the guy who literally wrote the book on somatic experiencing, talks about how trauma gets trapped in the body. He's spent decades showing that trauma isn't just a story in your head. It's a physiological event that your system never finishes processing. Your body stays in the fight or flight response. And your adrenals keep pumping out cortisol like there's a tiger in the room.

But there's no tiger. There hasn't been a tiger for years. Maybe decades. But your body doesn't know that. It's still trying to protect you from a threat that ended a long time ago. And your adrenals are paying the price.

The slow burn of chronic cortisol

Let me tell you what happens when cortisol runs high for too long. It's not pretty.

Cortisol is supposed to spike when you need it, then drop back down when the danger passes. That's how it works for people with regulated nervous systems. They get scared. They get stressed. They handle it. And then their body returns to baseline. Cortisol goes back to normal levels. The adrenals get a break.

But for those of us with complex trauma histories, that drop never happens. Or it happens briefly and then spikes again. And again. And again. Your adrenals are like a car engine that never turns off. They're running all the time. And eventually, something has to give.

What gives is your adrenal reserve. Your body's ability to produce cortisol when you actually need it. After years of overproduction, the adrenals start to fatigue. They can't keep up. And you enter what some researchers call adrenal insufficiency or, more colloquially, adrenal burnout.

This is where things get confusing. Because you might still feel wired. You might still have that buzzing anxiety in your chest. But your body is also deeply exhausted. You're running on fumes. You're in this weird state where you're both activated and depleted at the same time. It's like your foot is on the gas and the brake simultaneously. And your whole system is just grinding itself down.

Gabor Mate, who's written extensively about the connection between trauma and illness, talks about this directly. He's a physician who spent decades working with patients whose physical conditions were directly linked to unprocessed stress and trauma. His work shows that the body keeps score in ways we're only beginning to understand. A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link). It's not an easy read. But it might change how you see your own symptoms.

What adrenal burnout actually feels like

I want to be really specific here. Because a lot of people walk around with adrenal burnout and don't even know it. They think they're just lazy. Or depressed. Or not trying hard enough.

Here are some of the signs. See if any of these land for you.

You wake up tired. Even after eight or nine hours of sleep. You feel like you haven't rested at all. Your body is heavy. Your mind is foggy. And it takes hours to feel even remotely functional.

You crave salt and sugar. Constantly. Your body is trying to compensate for electrolyte imbalances caused by chronic stress hormone production. So you reach for chips. Or candy. Or both.

You get sick more often. And when you get sick, it takes forever to recover. Your immune system is compromised because your adrenals can't produce the cortisol needed to regulate inflammation properly.

You have low blood pressure. You get dizzy when you stand up too fast. Your body can't regulate its own systems anymore.

You feel wired but tired. That's the classic description. You're exhausted down to your bones, but your mind is racing. You can't sleep. Or you fall asleep but wake up at 3 AM and can't get back to rest.

You lose your capacity to handle stress. The small stuff feels huge. A minor inconvenience sends you into a tailspin. Your window of tolerance has shrunk to almost nothing.

Think about it. If your adrenals have been running on high for ten, twenty, thirty years, of course they're going to burn out. They were never designed for that kind of sustained output. They were designed for short bursts of activity followed by rest and recovery. That's the cycle. That's how it's supposed to work.

But trauma hijacks that cycle. It keeps you in the survival mode. And your body doesn't know the difference between a real threat and a remembered one. It responds the same way. Cortisol floods your system. Your heart rate increases. Your muscles tense. And your adrenals keep working.

Stay with me here. Because I know this sounds bleak. And it is. But understanding what's happening is the first step toward doing something about it.

How the nervous system keeps the cycle going

This is where polyvagal theory comes in. Stephen Porges developed this framework to explain how our nervous system responds to safety and danger. It's not just about fight or flight. There's a whole hierarchy of responses that your body cycles through.

When you're in a state of social engagement, you feel safe. You can connect with others. Your body is relaxed. Your adrenals are at baseline. This is where healing happens.

When you perceive threat, your sympathetic nervous system kicks in. That's the fight or flight response. Your adrenals start producing cortisol and adrenaline. Your body prepares for action.

And when the threat is overwhelming or inescapable, your dorsal vagal system takes over. That's the freeze response. Your body shuts down. You dissociate. You go numb.

For people with complex trauma, the nervous system gets stuck cycling between sympathetic activation and dorsal vagal shutdown. You're either anxious and wired or numb and checked out. There's very little time spent in that safe, regulated state where healing can actually occur.

And your adrenals are caught in the middle. They're being asked to produce stress hormones even when there's no real danger. They're being asked to keep you alert and ready even when your body desperately needs rest.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link). Deb Dana, who works closely with Porges, has done incredible work translating this science into something you can actually use. It's not just theory. It's a roadmap for your nervous system.

What recovery actually looks like

I'm not going to tell you there's a quick fix. There isn't. Adrenal burnout took years to develop, and it takes time to recover. But there are things that help.

The first thing is rest. Real rest. Not the kind where you're scrolling your phone or watching TV. The kind where you actually let your body down-regulate. Where you lie on the floor and breathe. Where you stop trying to be productive for a few minutes.

This is hard. Really hard. Because hypervigilance makes rest feel dangerous. Your body thinks that if you let your guard down, something bad will happen. So you have to slowly, carefully teach your nervous system that it's safe to rest. That you can let go. That the danger has passed.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link). Peter Levine writes about how the body holds trauma and how we can gently release it. It's not about forcing anything. It's about creating conditions for the nervous system to settle.

Nutrition matters too. Your adrenals need specific nutrients to function. Vitamin C, B vitamins, magnesium. These are depleted by chronic stress. You need to replenish them. And you need to eat regular meals. Your blood sugar is probably all over the place, and that puts more stress on your adrenals.

But here's what I want you to understand. Recovery isn't about fixing yourself. It's about giving your body what it's been missing. Safety. Rest. Connection. Predictability. Your adrenals didn't burn out because you're broken. They burned out because they were doing exactly what they were supposed to do. They were keeping you alive in an environment that felt dangerous.

The problem is that the danger is gone now. Or it's different. Or it's manageable. But your body doesn't know that yet. It's still operating from old data. And you have to help it update the map.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link). It's a classic in the trauma field for a reason. Levine explains how animals in the wild process and release stress, and how humans have lost that capacity. It's a different way of thinking about what's happening in your body.

An ending that isn't neat

I'm not going to tell you that if you just do these things, you'll be fine. That's the kind of toxic positivity I can't stand. The wellness industry loves to sell you a quick fix. Buy this supplement. Try this protocol. Do this breathing exercise. And all your problems will disappear.

It's not that simple. Adrenal burnout is real. And recovery is slow. And nonlinear. You'll have good days and bad days. Days where you feel almost normal. Days where you can barely get out of bed.

But here's what I can tell you. Your body is not your enemy. It's been trying to protect you this whole time. Your adrenals have been working themselves to exhaustion because they thought that's what you needed to survive. They were wrong. But they were trying to help.

And that matters. Because when you stop seeing your symptoms as evidence that you're broken, and start seeing them as evidence that your body has been fighting for you, something shifts. You stop fighting your body. You start working with it.

That's the real work. Not fixing. Not forcing. Just listening. And giving your body what it needs. Even if it takes a long time. Even if it's uncomfortable. Even if you don't know how.

You're not alone in this. And your body is not against you. It's been trying to save you. It's just been using old information. You can help it learn something new.

Healing Journey Resources

  • When the Body Says No (paid link) - Gabor Mate's exploration of the stress-illness connection
  • The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link) - Deb Dana's practical guide to nervous system regulation
  • In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) - Peter Levine on body-based trauma healing
  • Waking the Tiger (paid link) - Peter Levine's classic on somatic approaches to trauma
